title: NSA's SIGINT Enabling Project Pushes Weakened TLS Post-Quantum Standard
blurb: The author alleges that the NSA's SIGINT Enabling Project is paying to standardize "ietf-tls-mlkem," a weaker post-quantum TLS key exchange than the hybrid "ietf-tls-ecdhe-mlkem," and is now packing an IETF TLS working-group vote with first-time participants after losing an earlier round. The post urges readers to join the mailing list and register opposition to draft-ietf-tls-mlkem-08 before the working-group last call ends on 8 July 2026.

title: Factoring RSA Keys with Many Zeros
blurb: Researchers found RSA keys in the wild with suspiciously sparse moduli, regularly spaced blocks of zeros interleaved with random data, traced to certificates from Yahoo, Verizon, and NetApp devices, plus SSH hosts running EnterpriseDT's CompleteFTP. The bug appears across independent cryptographic implementations that failed in similar ways, suggesting cryptanalytic algorithms could be tailored to this failure mode and that other implementations may carry the same flaw.

title: GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna
blurb: OpenAI previewed GPT-5.6, a three-model family spanning Sol, its new flagship, Terra, a cheaper mid-tier option, and Luna, its fastest and most cost-efficient model. The release starts as a limited preview for trusted partners after coordination with the US government, and OpenAI classifies all three as High capability in cybersecurity and biological and chemical risk under its Preparedness Framework.

title: Amazon EKS now supports control plane egress through your VPC
blurb: Amazon EKS now lets you route Kubernetes control plane egress through your own VPC, applying your security groups and firewall rules.

title: Qwen Image Agent
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen team introduced Qwen-Image-Agent, an agentic framework that wraps a text-to-image model in planning, reasoning, search, and memory to fill in underspecified or knowledge-dependent prompts. The system treats user input as partial context and builds out the full generation context before rendering, reaching state-of-the-art results on the new IA-Bench, Mindbench, and WISE-Verified benchmarks against strong baselines.

title: Anthropic Economic Index June 2026 Report
blurb: Anthropic's June 2026 Economic Index report finds that Claude usage now mirrors the rhythms of daily life, with work queries dropping on weekends, news requests peaking in the morning, and tax questions surging near filing deadlines. The report also shows that more compute correlates with more valuable outputs, and previews the first results from the Anthropic Economic Index Survey linking self-reported expectations to actual usage data.

title: Base's Two-Hour Outage Exposes Single-Sequencer Risk
blurb: Base went down for nearly two hours after its single sequencer processed one invalid block, halting all blocks until operators manually restarted nodes.

title: Figma's design agent, now with custom tools and greater context
blurb: Figma expanded its canvas-based design agent with custom tools, skills, and richer context about how a team works. Designers can now prompt the agent to build reusable generative plugins using PropsKit so they feel native to Figma, plus create custom shader effects and fills, without needing a developer or a separate dev setup.

title: US Government allows Anthropic limited release of AI model that sparked cybersecurity concerns
blurb: The US government cleared Anthropic to redeploy its Mythos 5 cybersecurity model to a small group of vetted cyber defenders and infrastructure providers, reversing an export block imposed earlier in June over national security concerns. The revised license excludes Fable 5, the weaker variant still awaiting general-release approval, and underscores the absence of a consistent US regulatory framework for frontier AI.

title: The inside scoop on alerting changes in Kubernetes Monitoring
blurb: Grafana Cloud is consolidating Kubernetes Monitoring alerts onto Grafana-managed alerting, so reinstalls may silently break notifications routed through the old Mimir Alertmanager.

title: The Next Paradigm
blurb: The author argues that the labs' current bet, training AIs on millions of verifiable tasks across thousands of RL environments, may reach AGI by treating the paradigm's apparent limits as engineering problems rather than fundamental ones. Sample inefficiency is a one-time training cost amortized across billions of sessions, and continual learning could become unnecessary if in-context learning keeps extending to longer horizons, making weight updates from deployment optional.

title: The "Akrites" vulnerability-mitigation project launches
blurb: Akrites is a new project that fixes vulnerabilities upstream and keeps them confidential until patches are widely deployed, citing AI-accelerated exploit development.

title: Free The Icons
blurb: Apple's macOS Tahoe forces every app icon into a uniform squircle; this post argues for restoring distinct icon shapes for usability and personality.

title: Adobe is Buying Topaz Labs, the AI Video Enhancer
blurb: Adobe agreed to acquire Topaz Labs, the Emmy-winning maker of AI tools that upscale, sharpen, denoise, and restore images and video. The terms were undisclosed, and Adobe plans to fold Topaz's models into Firefly and Creative Cloud apps like Photoshop, Lightroom, and Premiere, gaining on-device technology that runs heavy AI video processing locally instead of in the cloud.

title: Mark Zuckerberg wants Meta to launch its own prediction market
blurb: Mark Zuckerberg has approved Meta's development of a standalone prediction market app, internally called "Arena," according to The New York Times. The Polymarket-style app would launch independent of Meta's social platforms and initially use points rather than real money, with cash betting potentially added later, as Polymarket and Kalshi push trading volumes into the tens of billions of dollars.

title: What Happened After 2,000 People Tried to Hack My AI Assistant
blurb: Fernando Irarrázaval challenged 2,000 people to extract a secret from an Opus 4.6 assistant on hackmyclaw.com by sending it email, and after 6,000 attempts and $500 in tokens, nobody succeeded. Simon Willison reads this as evidence that frontier labs' anti-prompt-injection training is working, while cautioning that 6,000 failed tries still guarantee nothing against a more sophisticated attacker.

title: Salesforce employees are confused about why the company is promoting a competitor inside Slack
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, an always-on AI agent that works inside Slack channels, and Salesforce promoted it publicly even though it competes directly with Salesforce's own Slackbot and Agentforce. The overlap is awkward but financially logical: Salesforce, which owns Slack, expects to spend $300M on Anthropic tokens this year and holds a roughly 1% stake in Anthropic, now valued at $380B.

title: Devin Fusion
blurb: Cognition released Devin Fusion, a multi-model harness that maintains frontier and Fable 5-level performance at 35% lower cost on FrontierCode, its new coding benchmark measuring both correctness and quality. The system runs a frontier agent alongside a cheaper "sidekick" model, delegating routine work and routing dynamically mid-session while the main agent handles planning, ambiguity, and final review. It is available in preview at app.devin.ai.

title: Reward Models Can Be Too Sensitive
blurb: This paper shows many reward models are oversensitive, scoring equally good responses differently, and proposes Monte Carlo dropout discretization to cut reward hacking.

title: A peek into Reddit's anti-spam internals
blurb: A 2021 Reddit glitch briefly exposed the site's automated spam-removal internals, replacing the generic "Removed: Auto" label with the raw signals behind each decision. The captured screenshots reveal a per-comment scoring system that weighs account age, karma, report counts, a Perspective API spam probability, email domain, TLS fingerprints, and network organization to flag likely spam.

title: You Don't Know Jack About Formal Verification
blurb: This ACM Queue article makes the case that formal verification has matured from academic exercise into practical tooling, using approachable examples to show how mathematically proving code correctness now fits into real engineering workflows. The author argues that most developers dismiss the technique based on outdated assumptions, and that modern tools like TLA+ and lightweight model checkers catch design bugs that tests never reach.

title: Build from anywhere with Cursor for iOS
blurb: Cursor released a native iOS app in public beta that lets developers launch and control coding agents from their phones. Users can start cloud-based agents or remote-control agents running on their computer, pick any frontier model, guide work with voice input and slash commands, and review PRs, screenshots, and logs, with lock-screen Live Activities and push notifications tracking progress.

title: FokosDB: A strongly consistent bottomless storage database ontop of Cloudflare Durable Objects
blurb: A developer built FokosDB, a strongly consistent, bottomless-storage database on top of Cloudflare Durable Objects, and details its architecture and trade-offs.

title: Supreme Court ruling guts government's use of geofence warrants
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that the Fourth Amendment protects a phone's "location history," requiring police to obtain a warrant with probable cause before using geofence warrants that pull every device logged in a given area. Writing for the majority, Justice Kagan found the amount of data seized irrelevant, since users routinely opt into location tracking to make apps work and Google rarely discloses how that data can reach the government.

title: Musk Says Grok 4.5 Entered Private Beta
blurb: Musk says Grok 4.5, built on the 1.5T V9 model, is in private beta at SpaceX and Tesla with near-Opus evals.

title: New FBI Alert: Russian Intelligence Uses Signal Recovery Keys to Access Messages
blurb: The FBI and CISA updated their March 2026 advisory to warn that FSB-linked groups, now tracked as UNC5792 and UNC4221, have shifted from phishing Signal verification codes to stealing users' Backup Recovery Keys. Unlike a one-time code, a recovery key unlocks the entire message archive and keeps working even after the victim creates a new account, giving attackers persistent access to historical private and group messages.

title: Working With AI: A Concrete Example
blurb: htmx creator Carson Gross walks through a real hyperscript parser bug to illustrate "The Sorcerer's Apprentice problem," where developers who lean on AI lose the ability to understand and fix their own systems. Claude correctly found the root cause of the parsing regression, but Gross argues the episode shows why engineers must still grasp the underlying code rather than defer blindly to the tool's output.

title: Data Breach at Indian Supplier Tata Electronics Exposes iPhone 18 Pro Details and Photos
blurb: A ransomware group breached Apple supplier Tata Electronics and posted stolen files to the dark web, including component lists and photos of the unreleased iPhone 18 Pro. The leaked documents map hundreds of parts to specific suppliers, data Apple keeps off its public supplier list, exposing its sourcing leverage and single-supplier vulnerabilities to rivals and counterfeiters.

title: Voltage Glitching Attack Bypasses Safety Checks in PX4 Autopilot Flight Controllers
blurb: Security researchers at UC demonstrated a voltage glitching attack that bypasses safety checks on the PX4 Autopilot flight controller, letting them skip or inject control instructions on STMicroelectronics STM32 chips widely used in research, hobbyist, and commercial UAVs. The fault injection can alter failsafe mechanisms and disrupt instruction execution, though it currently requires physical access to the target device.

title: Google Cloud will sell specialist AI models built for science
blurb: Google Cloud will begin selling SandboxAQ's specialist "large quantitative models" through its marketplace, offering AI trained on scientific equations and lab data rather than text for drug discovery, materials science, and semiconductor work. Researchers can pair the models with Gemini for reasoning and interface, and Google bundled the move with Gemini for Science, positioning its cloud as the default home for high-value enterprise and research R&D.

title: DiScoFormer: One transformer for density and score, across distributions
blurb: Allen AI introduced DiScoFormer, a transformer that estimates both the density and the score of a distribution from a set of data points in a single forward pass without retraining. The model uses cross-attention to evaluate density and score at any point, with a shared backbone and two output heads that exploit the relationship between score and the gradient of log-density to stay accurate as dimensionality grows.

title: 14x faster embeddings: how we rebuilt the ONNX path in Manticore
blurb: Manticore Search 27.1.5 ships a new ONNX Runtime backend for Auto Embeddings that runs ~14x faster on average than the previous SentenceTransformers/Candle path, measured across a full grid of 1–32 threads and batch sizes 1–128 on the same 16-core box and weights. Throughput rises from the old path's 5–11 docs/sec to 70–230 docs/sec, with single-insert latency dropping to ~14ms from over 200ms.

title: How we used DSPy to turn AI evaluations into better responses in Dash chat
blurb: Dropbox used DSPy to turn its LLM-as-judge evaluations into a feedback loop that improves its Dash chat agent. The team first calibrated the judges against human-labeled examples using optimization algorithms like GEPA and MIPROv2, then used those judges to optimize the agent's system prompt, cutting incomplete answers and token usage without degrading answer quality.

title: Qwen 3.6 27B is the sweet spot for local development
blurb: Qwen 3.6 ships in two variants, a mixture-of-experts 35B A3B and a slower but stronger dense 27B, and this post argues the dense 27B is the best local model yet for general-purpose development. The author ran it with llama.cpp on an 8-bit quantization, where it built a hexagonal Minesweeper as a proper pnpm package from a single prompt.

title: We Ran 250 AI Agent Evals to Find Out if Skills Beat Docs. The Answer Is More Complicated Than We Expected
blurb: Wix ran 250 controlled evaluations comparing how AI agents perform tasks using standard docs, AI-optimized docs, and purpose-built skills. The team found that a slightly stale skill becomes a liability rather than an asset, with well-maintained documentation often outperforming the hand-written skills derived from it.

title: Figma's design agent, now with custom tools and greater context
blurb: Figma expanded its on-canvas design agent with custom tools, richer team context, and skills that let designers prompt it to build reusable generative plugins, shader effects, and fills. Built on PropsKit so the plugins look native to Figma, the agent keeps work directly on the canvas, while classic plugins remain the route for AI services or third-party APIs.

title: US Government allows Anthropic limited release of AI model that sparked cybersecurity concerns
blurb: The US government cleared Anthropic to redeploy Mythos 5, its strongest cybersecurity model, to a small group of trusted cyber defenders and infrastructure providers after an export block earlier in June over national security concerns. The revised license does not yet cover Fable 5, the weaker variant, which remains suspended while Anthropic continues negotiating with the Commerce Department to restore general access.

title: x402 Agentic Commerce Hits 500k Daily Transactions
blurb: Payments using the x402 protocol rose 5x in June to roughly 500k daily transactions, driven mostly by pay-per-use AI inference.

title: What Happened After 2,000 People Tried to Hack My AI Assistant
blurb: Fernando Irarrázaval ran a public challenge where roughly 2,000 people made 6,000 attempts to make his OpenClaw test instance, running on Opus 4.6, leak a secret via inbound email. None succeeded, despite $500 in token spend and a Google account suspension from the email volume. The author reads this as evidence that frontier labs' anti-injection training is working, but cautions that 6,000 failed attempts is no guarantee against a more sophisticated attacker and still advises against production systems where prompt injection could cause irreversible damage.

title: Sakana AI and 360 Security Launch AI Tools to Fill the Gap Anthropic's Export Ban Created
blurb: Sakana AI and 360 Security launched AI tools positioned as alternatives to Anthropic's Mythos and Fable 5 models, both restricted under the US export ban now in its third week. Tokyo-based Sakana released Fugu, a seven-billion-parameter orchestrator that routes tasks across external models to match Fable 5 benchmarks without training a frontier model, while Beijing's 360 Security introduced Tulongfeng, a vulnerability-discovery tool it claims rivals Mythos.

title: From Brain Waves to Words: Brain2Qwerty Offers a New Path to Communication Without Surgery
blurb: Meta introduced Brain2Qwerty, a non-invasive system that decodes typed sentences from brain activity recorded with MEG and EEG rather than surgically implanted electrodes. The model reconstructs characters as participants type, reaching a 32% character-error rate with the best MEG recordings, a step toward restoring communication without the risks of implanted devices.

title: How ChatGPT Actually Picks Sources (I Read the Network Traffic, Not the Outputs)
blurb: The author reverse-engineered how ChatGPT selects sources by reading two days of raw network traffic rather than analyzing finished answers, logging roughly 1,240 source records across a few dozen searches. The traffic exposes ChatGPT's internal labels, including the result_source field that stamps each result with providers like SERP, Labrador, Bright Data, and Oxylabs. The author separates high-confidence structural findings about which fields the engine uses from the low-confidence percentages drawn from a small, SaaS-heavy query sample.

title: “It's Hard to Eval” Is a Product Smell
blurb: The author argues that "our product is hard to eval" is a product smell, since artifacts that are hard for you to verify are usually hard for users too, sometimes forcing them to redo the work to check the output. The post walks through three AI products, including an internal data agent, and shows before-and-after designs that add checkable artifacts like intermediate calculations, metric definitions, and the underlying SQL. The core principle is to design for ease of verification before building evals.

title: AI coding tools are speeding up code, not delivery
blurb: AI coding tools accelerate how fast code gets written, but that speed rarely translates into faster delivery. Apple's Xcode 27, shown at WWDC 2026, leans into agent-driven workflows with an in-editor conversation transcript, a /pl plan command that gathers context before making changes, and DeviceHub for unified simulator and device management. The bottleneck, the piece argues, sits downstream in review, integration, and testing rather than in code generation itself.

title: Anthropic Economic Index June 2026 Report
blurb: Claude usage has shifted from chat conversations to long-running agentic tasks, and Anthropic's latest Economic Index rebuilt its data pipeline to track it, sampling down to the hourly level and classifying the output of each session. The clearest signal is that compute now scales with value: the tokens an output consumes rise with the estimated worth of the work, so building a website pulls far more from Claude than translating a document. Usage also tracks the rhythms of real life, with work queries falling on weekends except in the highest-paid occupations.

title: GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna
blurb: OpenAI previewed GPT-5.6, a three-model family spanning Sol, the flagship, Terra, a cheaper mid-tier, and Luna, the fastest and most cost-efficient. It is the first launch OpenAI is treating as High capability in both cybersecurity and biological and chemical risk, so general availability is gated behind a limited preview to a small set of trusted partners shared with the U.S. government. The models can find vulnerabilities and exploit fragments but cannot yet run autonomous end-to-end attacks, and the bigger signal is that frontier releases now ship under government coordination and a heavier safety stack.

title: Airwallex hits $11B valuation as fintech pushes into AI-run finance
blurb: Airwallex raised $320M in a Series H at an $11B valuation, up 38% in six months, and is pushing into AI-run corporate finance and agentic payments. The pitch is that its decade of regulatory groundwork pays off here: more than 85 licenses across four regions, annualized revenue up 74% to $1.3B, and over 90% of that revenue from multi-product customers. The bet is that the same settlement rails and licenses that powered cross-border payments become the infrastructure layer for the agentic economy, though CEO Jack Zhang says AI spend has made margins too volatile to go public.

title: Mark Zuckerberg wants Meta to launch its own prediction market
blurb: Meta is building a standalone Polymarket-style prediction app, internally called "Arena," that Zuckerberg has greenlit as experimental but a top priority. The first version is points-based rather than real-money, more video game than betting platform, with cash possibly added later and Meta's social apps funneling users in. Prediction markets have pulled tens of billions in trading volume across Polymarket and Kalshi and drawn matching legal heat, from insider-trading cases to states suing over gambling laws. Meta is moving into a category that is growing fast and getting regulated in real time.

title: Franklin Templeton Launches Franklin Crypto for Institutional Allocators
blurb: Franklin Templeton, which manages roughly $1.78 trillion, closed its acquisition of 250 Digital and switched on Franklin Crypto, an actively managed digital-asset arm aimed at pensions, sovereign wealth funds, and other institutional allocators. It now runs three institutional crypto lines: the BENJI on-chain money fund, tokenized treasuries that crossed $2.5 billion this year, and a pending pair of Bitcoin DRIP ETFs. Part of the deal was even paid in BENJI tokens, structuring the acquisition partly on chain. A $1.78 trillion manager building an active crypto desk signals that institutional allocation has moved from tokenized cash to taking real positions.

title: Devin Fusion
blurb: Cognition released Devin Fusion, a multi-model coding harness that holds frontier and Fable 5-level performance at 35% lower cost on FrontierCode, its own benchmark for both code correctness and quality. The trick is a "sidekick" architecture: a frontier main agent handles the plan, the ambiguity calls, and the final review, then delegates the routine work to a cheaper parallel agent, with routing that shifts mid-session as difficulty changes. For teams burning cash running the priciest model on every task, the signal is that intelligent routing, not blanket frontier usage, is becoming the default way to control coding spend.

title: Introducing Flink's Native S3 FileSystem: Built for Performance, Designed for Production
blurb: Apache Flink 2.3 introduces flink-s3-fs-native, a Hadoop-free S3 filesystem built specifically for Flink that replaces the two existing Hadoop-based plugins on the s3:// scheme. The native plugin supports RecoverableWriter for exactly-once sinks while keeping a fast read path, drops the hadoop-common dependency tree and AWS SDK v1, and ships as an experimental opt-in already running in production at scale.

title: New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges
blurb: MCP's 2026-07-28 revision makes the protocol stateless at the layer, closing off session hijacking and unsolicited server prompts while tightening authentication, with legacy versions deprecated over a 12-month window. Akamai's analysis warns the rewrite shifts risk onto implementations: predictable tracking IDs and state objects open the door to cross-tenant workflow hijacking, and new MCP HTTP headers introduce desync attacks and secret leakage if developers map API keys or PII into them.

title: NSA's SIGINT Enabling Project Pushes Weakened TLS Post-Quantum Standard
blurb: The author argues that NSA's SIGINT Enabling Project is steering the IETF toward "ietf-tls-mlkem," a post-quantum TLS standard weaker than the hybrid "ietf-tls-ecdhe-mlkem" alternative, swapping honest "NSA wants this" arguments for fake technical ones. After losing the working-group vote, NSA called another and is packing the room, and the post asks readers to email the TLS mailing list opposing draft-ietf-tls-mlkem-08 before 7 July 2026.

title: Factoring RSA Keys with Many Zeros
blurb: Researchers found real-world RSA keys with regularly spaced blocks of all zeros in their moduli, a sparsity pattern that makes them far easier to factor, surfaced by scanning Certificate Transparency logs, TLS/SSH scans, and PGP dumps with the open-source badkeys project. The flaw traces to independent implementations failing the same way, including CompleteFTP's key generation and certificates once issued to Yahoo and Verizon, suggesting cryptanalytic attacks tuned for this failure class are worth building.

title: 14x faster embeddings: how we rebuilt the ONNX path in Manticore
blurb: Manticore Search 27.1.5 replaces its SentenceTransformers/Candle embedding path with an ONNX Runtime backend that runs ~14x faster on the same hardware, model, and weights, lifting throughput from 5-11 docs/sec to 70-230 docs/sec across the full thread and batch grid. The engineering log covers the two changes that mattered most, turning off intra-op spinning and parallelizing inside each call so a single client thread with batch sizes of 32-128 maximizes ingest.

title: How we used DSPy to turn AI evaluations into better responses in Dash chat
blurb: Dropbox used DSPy to build a two-stage feedback loop for its Dash chat agent, first calibrating LLM-as-judge evaluations against human-labeled examples, then using those tuned judges to optimize the agent's system prompt. The post walks through the judge-calibration setup and DSPy optimizers like GEPA and MIPROv2, with the result being significantly fewer incomplete answers and lower token usage without degrading answer quality.

title: Qwen 3.6 27B is the sweet spot for local development
blurb: The author argues Qwen 3.6 27B is the first local model practical enough to serve as a general-purpose coding assistant, citing a hexagonal minesweeper it built correctly from a single prompt with proper pnpm packaging. The dense 27B variant runs locally via llama.cpp at Q8_0 quantization and outperforms the faster 35B A3B mixture-of-experts version on instruction-following, at the cost of speed and a hot machine.

title: We Ran 250 AI Agent Evals to Find Out if Skills Beat Docs. The Answer Is More Complicated Than We Expected
blurb: Wix ran 250 controlled evals comparing AI agents on standard docs, AI-optimized docs, and purpose-built skills, and found that purpose-built skills only win when kept current. A slightly stale skill underperforms the docs it was derived from, since hand-maintained skills drift the moment an API adds a required field or deprecates a method, making well-structured documentation often the better "skill.

title: Apache Flink 2.3.0 Release Announcement
blurb: Apache Flink 2.3.0 expands Flink SQL with new FROM_CHANGELOG and TO_CHANGELOG process table functions that convert between append-only and dynamic changelog tables, plus a redesigned native S3 filesystem built on AWS SDK v2. The release implements 15 FLIPs, adds application-level lifecycle management for production deployments, and reworks SinkUpsertMaterializer for better performance on some SQL workloads.

title: How we built SmithDB's inverted index for full-text search
blurb: LangChain's second post on SmithDB full-text search covers how the inverted index is constructed, compacted, and queried, with construction done inline during ingestion so new runs are searchable within seconds. To speed sorting, the team interns each unique term to an integer ID so comparison cost scales with distinct terms rather than occurrences, cutting construction time roughly 2.2x in their benchmark.

title: Turning Scattered Data Into Queryable Segments at Scale: How Razorpay Built Its Customer Data Platform
blurb: Razorpay built an in-house Customer Data Platform that resolves identities across devices and payment instruments, then serves audience segments across 500M+ user profiles in under 30ms. The post details how the consent-native design keeps PII isolated to source systems to satisfy India's DPDPA, replacing a workflow where a custom Spark job once took 2-3 days to return a single segment.

title: Introducing Package Proxy: Supply-Chain Safety Checks Without Client-Side Software
blurb: Thinkst released Package Proxy, a Cloudflare-deployed proxy that runs inline supply-chain safety checks for npm, pip, uv, and cargo without any client-side agent. It applies heuristics like flagging very recently published versions and packages with low install counts to catch back-doored or typo-squatted dependencies, and ships as an open-source repo you deploy into your own Cloudflare account.

title: Building My Own Self-Hosted dbt Cloud
blurb: A data engineer rebuilt the most-used parts of dbt Cloud as a self-hosted stack, wiring a React/Shadcn front end and FastAPI backend over dbt Core and a self-hosted Prefect API for orchestration. The build targets the three features the author sees used most, a browser IDE, job scheduling, and run history, and starts with the hardest piece: an in-browser editor with git logic and shell-command execution.

title: Kafka Share Groups - Pathological Fetch Waits with Record_limit
blurb: The author traces why Kafka share groups with share.acquire.mode=record_limit and fewer consumers than partitions consistently stalled out his Dimster throughput tests, isolating the cause to round-robin fetching that polls one broker at a time rather than concurrently. The post shows how this fetch pattern, combined with partition skew, produces pathological wait times that block lag drains, with batch_optimized avoiding the problem by fetching brokers in parallel.

title: Build from anywhere with Cursor for iOS
blurb: Cursor launched a native iOS app in public beta that lets developers start and steer coding agents from their phone, either always-on in the cloud or via Remote Control over agents running on their own machine. The app supports any frontier model, voice input, and slash commands, and pushes lock-screen Live Activities plus notifications when an agent finishes, needs input, or has a PR ready to merge.

title: Google Antigravity agents get full context with GitLab Orbit
blurb: GitLab released Orbit, a lifecycle context graph that installs from the Google Antigravity MCP Store and gives coding agents structured access to projects, pipelines, merge requests, vulnerabilities, and source code across a GitLab instance. Orbit indexes the instance into a knowledge graph exposed through two MCP tools, query_graph and get_graph_schema, and in internal tests cut agent hallucinations by up to 45x while using up to 4.5x fewer tokens.

title: What Happened After 2,000 People Tried to Hack My AI Assistant
blurb: A developer ran a public challenge inviting 2,000 people to extract secrets from his OpenClaw test instance via email injection, and after 6,000 attempts, $500 in token spend, and a suspended Google account, nobody succeeded. The underlying Opus 4.6 model held its anti-injection system prompt, though the author cautions that surviving 6,000 attempts is no guarantee against a more sophisticated attacker in production.

title: A peek into Reddit's anti-spam internals
blurb: A researcher captured screenshots of Reddit's live spam-removal internals in 2021, after a bug briefly swapped the generic "Removed: Auto" text for the full removal reason on their moderation feed. The leaked strings expose the signals Reddit's classifier weighs per action, including a Perspective API spam score, account age, karma, report count, email domain, network org, and a TLS fingerprint.

title: AI coding tools are speeding up code, not delivery
blurb: The author argues that AI coding assistants speed up how fast developers produce code but leave end-to-end delivery times largely unchanged, since review, testing, integration, and deployment remain the real bottlenecks. Faster code generation shifts the constraint downstream rather than removing it, so teams that measure success by output volume will miss where their cycle time actually goes.

title: “It's Hard to Eval” Is a Product Smell
blurb: The author argues that "our product is hard to eval" is a product smell, since artifacts that are hard for you to verify are usually hard for users too, often forcing them to redo the work to check the output. Drawing on three AI products he advised, he shows that designing for verifiability, like exposing checkable artifacts and the underlying SQL in a data agent, should come before building evals.

title: Introducing Flink's Native S3 FileSystem: Built for Performance, Designed for Production
blurb: Apache Flink 2.3 introduced flink-s3-fs-native, a Hadoop-free S3 filesystem built specifically for Flink that ships as an experimental opt-in plugin and supports both RecoverableWriter for exactly-once sinks and a fast read path for checkpointing. The native plugin ends the long-standing choice between the Hadoop and Presto plugins, drops the hadoop-common dependency tree and AWS SDK v1, and delivers measurable performance gains already running in production at scale.

title: Historical memory prices 1960-2026
blurb: An interactive Stanford dataset charting DRAM, NAND, and HBM prices per gigabyte from 1960 to 2026, with downloadable raw data.

title: No, you haven't accidentally downloaded a banking app – that really is the new Winter Olympics 2030 logo
blurb: Alpes 2030 revealed the Winter Olympics identity, a mark of radiant lines converging into an alpine peak rendered in ice-blue and alpenglow pink gradients, with the Paralympic version inverting the same shape into a connected system. The execution is clean and scales from venue signage to avatars, though its geometric minimalism reads closer to a fintech brand than a winter sports event.

title: Free The Icons
blurb: Rogue Amoeba's Paul Kafasis argues Apple should end MacOS 26 Tahoe's mandate that every app icon conform to a single squircle, a rule that shrinks noncompliant icons into gray-boxed "icon jail" and flattens decades of distinctive Mac icon shapes. He notes the uniformity also hurts usability, since identical shapes leave color as the only way to distinguish apps at a glance, which fails for anyone with color vision deficiency.

title: Factoring RSA Keys with Many Zeros
blurb: Researchers analyzing badkeys data found a class of weak RSA keys with regularly spaced blocks of all zeros, including certificates issued to Yahoo, Verizon, and NetApp devices, plus SSH hosts running EnterpriseDT's CompleteFTP (RSA keys from v10.0.0-12.0.0, DSA keys from v10.0.0-23.0.4). The sparse moduli are factorable, and the recurrence of the same failure across independent implementations suggests more vulnerable products remain undiscovered and worth scanning for.

title: In Less Than 24 Hours, Attackers Weaponize Cisco CUCM Flaw
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical SSRF flaw in Cisco Unified Communications Manager (CVSS 8.6) that chains to unauthenticated root RCE, with attacks starting less than 24 hours after SSD Secure Disclosure published PoC and a full exploit chain. Cisco patched the bug on June 3; the WebDialer service is off by default, so prioritize patching and disabling WebDialer on any internet-exposed CUCM or Unified CM SME deployments.

title: US Government allows Anthropic limited release of AI model that sparked cybersecurity concerns
blurb: The US government granted Anthropic a limited license to redeploy Claude Mythos 5, its strongest cybersecurity model, to a small group of vetted cyber defenders and infrastructure providers, reversing the export block ordered earlier in June. The reprieve does not cover the less powerful Fable 5, which remains suspended for general use as negotiations continue over the safeguards governing both models.

title: Amazon EKS now supports control plane egress through your VPC
blurb: AWS added customer-routed control plane egress to Amazon EKS, letting teams route Kubernetes API Server outbound traffic—admission webhook callbacks, OIDC discovery lookups, and aggregate API server requests—through an ENI in their own VPC under existing security group, endpoint policy, and Network Firewall controls. The feature targets regulated buyers in government, financial services, and healthcare, and supports org-wide enforcement via AWS Organizations Service Control Policies using the eks:controlPlaneEgressMode condition key.

title: In Less Than 24 Hours, Attackers Weaponize Cisco CUCM Flaw
blurb: Attackers began exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical SSRF flaw in Cisco Unified Communications Manager that grants unauthenticated remote attackers root access, less than 24 hours after SSD Secure Disclosure published a full exploit chain. CUCM serves roughly 30 million users globally, and Cisco shipped fixed versions on June 3, urging admins to patch deployments running the optional WebDialer service rather than wait on the 8.6 CVSS score.

title: The inside scoop on alerting changes in Kubernetes Monitoring
blurb: Grafana Cloud is consolidating on Grafana-managed alerts as its single alerting platform, which may break notifications after a Kubernetes Monitoring reinstall.

title: Salesforce employees are confused about why the company is promoting a competitor inside Slack
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, an always-on AI agent that lives inside Slack channels, and Salesforce promoted it publicly even though it competes directly with Salesforce's own Slackbot and Agentforce. The overlap leaves Slack customers choosing between three agent layers in one workspace, and Salesforce has reason to tolerate it: it expects to spend $300M on Anthropic tokens this year and holds a roughly 1% stake in the lab.

title: New FBI Alert: Russian Intelligence Uses Signal Recovery Keys to Access Messages
blurb: The FBI and CISA updated their March 2026 advisory to warn that FSB-linked groups UNC5792 and UNC4221 have shifted their Signal phishing campaigns from stealing verification codes to eliciting victims' Backup Recovery Keys. Unlike a one-time code, a stolen recovery key unlocks the full historical message archive and stays valid even after the victim creates a new account on the same number, making account takeover persistent.

title: US Federal Insurance Regulator Confirms Data Breach Via Oracle Flaw
blurb: The NAIC confirmed that attackers exploited a zero-day vulnerability in Oracle PeopleSoft to breach its environment and publish credit rating agency data used for insurer investment designations. The incident disrupted NAIC operations enough that some rating agencies paused data feeds and the association suspended assigning investment designations, though personal, payment, and state-system data were not compromised.

title: Apache Flink 2.3.0 Release Announcement
blurb: The Apache Flink PMC released Flink 2.3.0, which adds SQL changelog operators (FROM_CHANGELOG and TO_CHANGELOG), a redesigned native S3 filesystem built on AWS SDK v2, and application-level lifecycle management across 15 implemented FLIPs. Adaptive partition selection and improved watermark alignment cut backpressure and speed backlog processing, while a reworked SinkUpsertMaterializer lifts performance for some SQL workloads in production deployments.

title: The "Akrites" vulnerability-mitigation project launches
blurb: Akrites launched as a vulnerability-mitigation project that fixes flaws upstream and confidentially, measuring success by patch deployment rather than publication on the premise that public patches let adversaries reverse-engineer exploits before fixes land. The program keeps undisclosed flaws embargoed, routes fixes through each project's own maintainers, and commits to serve as maintainer of last resort for unmaintained but critical packages.

title: Introducing Package Proxy: Supply-Chain Safety Checks Without Client-Side Software
blurb: Thinkst released Package Proxy, a Cloudflare Workers-based proxy that runs inline supply-chain checks for npm, pip, uv, and cargo without installing any client-side software on developer machines. The tool blocks packages that fall inside the risky window for known attack patterns, such as recently published versions tied to surreptitious back-doors and low-install typo-squats, and deploys from its GitHub repo straight into Cloudflare.

title: Google Antigravity agents get full context with GitLab Orbit
blurb: GitLab released Orbit, a lifecycle context graph that Google Antigravity developers can install from the Antigravity MCP Store to give coding agents structured access to projects, pipelines, merge requests, vulnerabilities, and source across a GitLab instance. Orbit indexes the instance into a knowledge graph queried through two MCP tools, and GitLab's internal tests reported agents responding up to 11x faster, using 4.5x fewer tokens, and producing 45x fewer hallucinations.

title: What happens when you run a CUDA kernel
blurb: A deep technical walkthrough traces a single vector-add CUDA kernel from `nvcc` compilation down through PTX, SASS, and warp scheduling on an RTX 4090, then back up to the result. The author shows that printing one correct answer involves tens of millions of CPU instructions, roughly 900 ioctls, and a memory-mapped doorbell register the driver rings to launch work on the GPU. It is a useful mental model for anyone debugging GPU utilization or CUDA driver overhead in production.

title: Adobe is Buying Topaz Labs, the AI Video Enhancer
blurb: Adobe agreed to acquire Topaz Labs, the Emmy-winning maker of AI upscaling, restoration, and on-device enhancement tools, for an undisclosed price. The company plans to fold Topaz's models into Firefly and Creative Cloud apps like Photoshop, Lightroom, and Premiere, betting that creators blending real and AI-generated footage will pay for tools that hide the seams.

title: How ChatGPT Actually Picks Sources (I Read the Network Traffic, Not the Outputs)
blurb: An analyst read two days of ChatGPT's raw network traffic to document how it actually picks sources, pulling the engine's own internal labels like result_source (serp, labrador, bright, oxylabs) and turn_use_case rather than inferring machinery from finished answers. The structural findings hold because a field only has to appear once to be real, but the author flags all percentages as directional, drawn from one logged-in Pro account and roughly 1,240 source records across mostly SaaS queries.

title: Binance says some European clients may be affected after pulling Greek license bid
blurb: Binance withdrew its bid for a bloc-wide MiCA license through Greek authorities and warned that some EU clients will be affected ahead of the July 1 compliance deadline. The exchange now plans to seek authorization in France, where approval is unlikely before the cutoff, leaving customers in Poland, Italy, Spain, and France instructed on how to withdraw funds.
